essential_question: Once you can measure an angle to the exact degree, what do you call it — and what happens when two angles are added together?
objective: Classify an angle as acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex from its degree measure, and find the resulting type when two angle measures are added.

content.prompt: Picture a door swinging open from fully closed, all the way until it lies flat against a wall — and then imagine a strong hinge that keeps going past that. How many different 'kinds' of angle do you think that one swing passes through, and where do you think the swing changes name?

agenda.title: I do — Five Families, Sorted by Degree

content.heading: Every angle belongs to exactly one family
bullets: Acute: greater than 0° and less than 90° — a narrow, 'tight' opening.
Right: exactly 90° — a perfect square corner, marked with a small square, never an arc.
Obtuse: greater than 90° and less than 180° — wider than square, not yet flat.
Straight: exactly 180° — two opposite rays forming one unbroken line.
Reflex: greater than 180° and less than 360° — bigger than straight, the 'far side' of an angle.
Add two angle measures like plain numbers, then reclassify the sum: right + acute = obtuse; right + right = straight; straight + acute = reflex.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag the Ray, Watch the Type

content.heading: Turn the ray all the way around; watch the family change
content.note: On the sim, drag the gold handle around the full circle. Watch m∠AVB and its type update live — the arc's color always matches the classification. Land exactly on 90° and 180°, then keep going past 180° into reflex. Switch to 'Match the Type' and try to land in a named family.

content.preview: Build session: hunt real angles of each type and build a reflex angle with a hinge or a clock face.

success_criteria.text: I can classify an angle as acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex from its degree measure
I can find the resulting angle type when two angle measures are added (e.g., right + acute = obtuse)
I can explain why 90° and 180° each get their own exact name instead of falling under acute, obtuse, or reflex

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W05 Types of Angles.md

questions.prompt: An angle measuring exactly 90° is a(n)…
questions.explain: 90° is the one exact boundary between acute and obtuse, so it gets its own name: right.

questions.prompt: Which of these degree measures describes an obtuse angle?
questions.explain: Obtuse is strictly between 90° and 180°; 135° falls in that open range, while 90° and 180° are their own named types.

questions.prompt: The figure shows ∠AVB. Measure it with a real protractor to the nearest whole degree, then classify it as acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex.
questions.explain: Rubric: a measured value of about 23°–27° (print/measurement tolerance) plus the correct family — any value strictly between 0° and 90° is acute.

questions.prompt: A straight angle (180°) plus an acute angle equals a(n) ___ angle.
questions.explain: 180° plus any amount greater than 0° is more than 180° and less than 360° (as long as the acute part stays under 180°) — that range is reflex.

questions.prompt: Explain why 90° is classified as its own type, 'right,' rather than being called acute or obtuse.
questions.answer: Acute is defined as strictly less than 90°, and obtuse is defined as strictly greater than 90°; 90° itself sits exactly on that boundary, so it belongs to neither open range and gets its own exact name and mark instead.
questions.explain: Full credit: names the strict (open) inequality on both sides of 90° and says the boundary value gets its own category.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw an obtuse angle. Label the vertex and both rays, and write its degree measure next to it.
questions.answer: Any angle drawn strictly between 90° and 180°, with the vertex and both rays labeled and the measure written beside it.
questions.explain: Rubric: a vertex with two labeled rays (1 pt) + a written degree measure that is honestly between 90° and 180° when checked with a protractor (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Exit: find one angle around you right now and classify it (acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex). Defend your answer using its degree range.
